title: Italian rainbow cookies
subtitle: Three almond sponge layers in red, white, and green with jam and chocolate
excerpt: Three thin layers of almond paste sponge, tinted in the colours of the Italian flag, sandwiched with apricot jam, pressed overnight under a weight to compress, then cut into squares and enrobed in dark chocolate.
sourceNotes: Italian rainbow cookies (also called Venetian cookies, tricolore cookies, or seven-layer cookies) are an Italian-American creation rather than a dish from Italy itself. They emerged in the early to mid-twentieth century among Italian immigrant communities in New York and are associated with Italian-American bakeries. The three-layer almond sponge enrobed in chocolate is now a standard fixture of Italian-American pastry shops. The recipe here follows the classic Italian-American bakery approach.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze assembled and enrobed, cut into cookies. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The assembled uncoated block must be pressed overnight under a weight in the fridge before cutting and coating. The finished cookies keep for a week in an airtight container.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dense, moist sponge made with finely ground almonds or almond paste (marzipan) in place of most of the flour. The fat content of the almonds produces a tight, fudgy crumb that holds together under pressure and slices cleanly. It differs from a standard fatless sponge: it does not spring back when pressed and is intended to be dense.

The assembled, sandwiched layers are wrapped tightly in cling film and weighted in the fridge overnight. The pressure compresses the layers so they adhere firmly. A tin filled with tins of food works well as a weight. Without pressing, the layers separate when cut.

content.text: Rainbow cookies are a project, not an afternoon bake. They require three separately coloured layers of
, each baked in the same tin, sandwiched with jam, and then
before enrobing in chocolate. Do not rush the pressing step: the compressed block must be completely firm before cutting or the layers will pull apart.

Preheat the oven to 175°C conventional. Grease and line a 20 cm square tin. Beat the marzipan, butter, and sugar together until smooth. Beat in the eggs one at a time. Fold in the flour, baking powder, and salt. Divide the batter into three equal portions. Leave one plain (white), colour one red, and one green. Bake each layer separately in the prepared tin for 10 to 12 minutes until set and barely golden. Slide each layer onto a rack to cool, using the paper to lift it out. Keep the paper under each layer while cooling.

Place the green layer on a board and brush with a thin layer of warm apricot jam. Place the white layer on top and brush again with jam. Top with the red layer. Wrap tightly in cling film, place on a flat tray, and press with a heavy weight overnight in the fridge.

Melt the dark chocolate. Unwrap the pressed block. Spread melted chocolate over the top and all four sides. Set on a rack until the top coat is firm. Flip, coat the base, and allow to set completely. Using a long sharp knife, trim the edges, then cut into strips and across into cookies approximately 4 cm wide. Wipe the knife clean between cuts.
